data.title: Hershey: Not Sweet Enough Given The Rising Structural Risks
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered The Hershey Company (HSY), I highlighted how despite some improvements and solid fundamentals, the valuation remained fair, which is not enough to justify buying it from my point of view given the potential risks.

Although the company remains in a solid financial position and could benefit from a potential macro recovery, the ongoing pressure and long-term fundamental threats shouldn’t be ignored, which is why HSY remains a Hold, as a better margin of safety is needed.

Fundamentals Remain Solid

HSY reported a solid Q4 and 2025 overall, beating the market's top- and bottom-line estimates significantly and offering better guidance than expected, with the growth in pricing helping to more than offset the volume/mix.

The free cash flow reached a solid $1.82 billion (slightly below the $1.93 billion seen in 2024), as the drop in CAPEX helped offset the macro pressure to some degree, while the year also came with the major acquisition of LesserEvil, growing their exposure to the high-growth better-for-you snack category.

HSY also announced unifying its sweet, salty and protein portfolios into ONE, which they say would help bring more innovation, flavors, variety, etc., combining the execution of their commercial activity and centralizing their global brand marketing.

As for the guidance, the company expects a solid 4% to 5% growth in net sales (with about 150 b.p. coming from the LesserEvil acquisition), with the Adjusted EPS growing by 30% to 35% (mostly thanks to a mark-to-market loss unwinding, margin recovery and a normalization of tax effects), while CAPEX would reach between $425 million to $475 million, similar to last year's $454.62 million.

Financially, based on HSY's latest report, they remain in a solid position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities, and even though the debt is growing to fund their expansion, their maturities remain spread very well, with over $925 million in cash and cash equivalents offering a lot of flexibility.

Given the potential for macro improvements and the solid balance sheet, we can certainly see how their idea of 2026 being a year to build back margins can be true, with more detail to come during their Investor Day at the end of March:

(...) we are balancing our commitment to achieving the full profit potential of our business with our focus on long-term sustainable growth and strong margins. In 2026, you will see us increase our budgets for R&D, innovation, brand building and our retail sales team. We will talk more about these and expected returns at our Investor Day in March. Advertising and related consumer spending is expected to increase double digits versus the prior year as we support momentum across our business. Investment will be up across both working and nonworking media as we develop creative in support of our long-term brand and portfolio objectives.

The company's ~2.73% dividend was recently increased by 6% compared to a year ago, representing about $1.15 billion, meaning a payout ratio of roughly 63% based on last year's cash flow, which is not bad, but also not an amazing yield compared to others in the industry.

Mixed Macro Signals

The recent Supreme Court decision (regardless of the increase to 15% that followed it) was a solid development overall, potentially helping the environment and offering some relief for the consumer from the pressure caused by the higher tariff rates, with the previous exemption reportedly carrying over and helping cocoa prices continue their decline, although they're still trading at relatively elevated levels.

That’s important especially given the significant levels of consumer pressure, which caused trouble in the US in recent years even for companies that were considered much more defensive, which came as a result of a mix between the ongoing high-rate environment combined with these tariffs and now the Iran conflict.

We can already see the significant jumps in gas prices, and this conflict can certainly lead to a lot worse the longer it lasts for, which can be terrible for HSY and its consumers.

The latter already caused the Fed to warn about the uncertainty seen in the economy, recently deciding not to cut the rates and signaling the potential of a single rate cut later this year, causing even more fear for an already weak market.

Long-Term Risks Persist

However, although Hershey has some of the most popular brands in their categories, we should keep in mind that these trade-downs and macro pressure can still affect their brands, as retailers might be the biggest long-term winners from this thanks to their private label brands.

Not only can they gain the trust of consumers and build these brands as a result of the significant trade-downs, but they also gather excellent amount of valuable data that can allow them to tailor their portfolio based on consumer trends, potentially capturing the consumer's demand for higher price points when the consumer's budget recovers (e.g. launching a Premium/Deluxe/Supreme/etc. version of the in-house brand).

This goes together with the potential impact from GLP-1 medicine, which was shown to lead to drops of ~10% in grocery spending for certain sweets and snacks, which can affect Hershey at its core, with the company recently calling it a potential demand headwind.

Still, given their strong brands and potential to focus on higher-protein, lower-calorie and other trends combined with the fact that the adoption of this medicine will realistically not be 100%, I don't expect the impact to be something they can't manage, and especially given their potential to expand internationally, I believe they can potentially more than mitigate these risks, which are likely going to be much bigger threats for lower-price-point products that can be easily replaced.

However, this can also be accentuated by HSY's moves (likely) in an effort to preserve/improve their margins to the detriment of their quality, which Brad Reese, the grandson of the famous recipe's inventor also highlighted recently. It's true that the current environment is tough for everyone and they aren't the only ones downgrading/replacing ingredients or “right-sizing” their offering, but it's these practices that make consumers gradually lose their trust, which can have detrimental effects during a potential recovery and in the longer term.

Valuation

For HSY’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start from last year’s $1.82 billion FCF and assume a CAGR of 8% for the first 5 years (meaning ~$1.97 billion in 2026) thanks to the potential macro improvements and margin developments, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Note that I won't adjust for net borrowings since their portfolio remains in a solid state, without needing to do significant adjustments.

I’m going to use a conservative discount rate of 9.5% to highlight the competitive risk and potential fundamental pressure but also balanced by their solid financials and brands, with 203.27 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $36.40 billion, meaning an intrinsic value of $179.08 per share, which continues to be above the current $210.58.

Conclusions

HSY remains a Hold, as even though their financials remain solid given the cash flow and balance sheet, the ongoing macro developments are far from great, while their quality concerns combined with other fundamental threats should make investors demand a higher margin of safety than the current levels.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Can We Get Through 2026
data.description:
The Federal Reserve added $124.1 billion to its securities portfolio from November 26, 2025, to March 18, 2026.

The round of "quantitative tightening" ended around December 1, 2025, and a new round of "quantitative easing" began.

Since the banking week just before December 1, 2025, ended on November 26, 2025, this represents the closest thing we have to a "starting date" for collecting data for the "new" period of quantitative easing.

This is a fairly sizeable increase in securities held outright by the Fed and includes the three-week time period that can be related to the start of the "war" in the Middle East...in Iran.

Again, we can't associate the Fed's data exactly with the start of the "war" because the Fed's statistical week always begins on a Wednesday.

So, the closest timing we have to the start of the "war" is the banking week beginning February 25, 2026.

Since February 25, 2026, the portfolio of securities held outright at the Federal Reserve has increased by $36.9 billion.

Again, this is a fairly sizeable increase for this time period considered.

Here is what the chart looks like.

Just looking at these data, I don't think that one can really say that the Federal Reserve has "picked up" its acquisitions of securities in the last three weeks.

It is just this result I am looking for.

My feeling has been that one of the reasons that President Trump started the "war" was to get the federal government spending, and spending in a way that the Federal Reserve would need to support these purchases by acquiring a lot of the debt securities created by this added "war" expenditure.

I have expressed this idea in my blog post "The Trump Effort is Finding A Way."

But, whereas I cannot say assuredly that this rise in the Fed's securities holdings appears to support the idea of a "new" Federal Reserve effort to add securities to its portfolio... I think I am safe in saying that the rise in the securities portfolio in the February 25 to March 18 time period does not indicate that the Fed DID NOT increase the securities portfolio to support the government spending on the "new" war effort.

Let's just say at this time that the movement of the Fed's holdings of securities held outright does not let us claim that the Fed did not increase its holdings of securities held outright to support Trump's "war" effort.

We must continue to watch the data to draw a conclusion on this issue.

Whatever, the Federal Reserve has increased its holdings of securities since the Fed moved to a position of quantitative easing at the start of December 2025.

I believe that one can say that the "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system, measured by the line item "Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve Banks," did increase during this period.

If the government had to go into the market and raise funds to support the "war" effort, this, one would suppose, would put pressure on short-term interest rates to rise.

If the Federal Reserve acted in order to keep short-term interest rates from rising by increasing "excess reserves," this is exactly what one would expect to happen.

If one looks at the yields on longer-term Treasury issues during March, one can see that there has been pressure on these longer-term securities to rise.

Again, this tends to support the argument that the Federal Reserve did indeed make some move after the "war" was started to keep interest rates from rising, and it did this by increasing, more rapidly, its securities portfolio.

Remember, the Fed had already begun quantitative easing. Now it looks like one could say that the Fed also reacted to market conditions and added more securities to its portfolio in order to keep short-term interest rates from rising further.

That is, the Fed moved to help the Treasury Department finance the debt that was being issued to support the new "war" effort.

We still need more information to support that this really happened. The "book" is not closed yet.

The money stock is still showing "positive" gains...but not too rapidly.

One could argue that the economy still needs to see the money stock growing in order to support rising real economic growth.

As I have written elsewhere, the majority of economists still seem to believe that real economic growth in 2026 will come in within the 2.0% to 2.5% range.

This would be a good, solid performance in this current world of uncertainty and disarray.

Although there is still a lot that must be done in the economy...relative to inflation and to the income/wealth distribution...to have the real economy continue on at a 2.0%-to-2.5% pace would be a real plus.

At least, this amount of economic growth would be very helpful and provide a "platform" for the Federal Reserve...and the federal government...to keep building the American economy of the future.

The bottom line for this report is that things are not really bad, although they are not really good either.

But, it looks as if economic growth could continue to support things and provide a foundation for positive things in the future.

data.title: Toronto-Dominion Bank Is Still Fundamentally Resilient But Almost Fully Priced
data.description:
Only two months after the publication of my previous coverage, Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD) has already shown some selling pressures, which justifies my rating downgrade to hold. Fundamentally, TD is a good defensive and dividend stock to retain in your portfolio if you already have it. But if you’re a newcomer, I suggest you stay on the sidelines and wait for a better entry point. Macroeconomic headwinds and valuation also suggest caution as the stock remains fully priced. Technicals adhere to it as the price trend reflect its potential consolidation.

TD Q1 2026: Income Stability Amid Market Volatility

Macroeconomic conditions in the US and Canada are still volatile despite the recent policy cycle by the Fed and Bank of Canada, or simply Boc. Inflation remains sticky as prices keep increasing, exacerbated by tariff wars. With that, even a Canadian Big Five bank like Toronto-Dominion Bank is not completely shielded due to its cyclical nature and sensitivity to economic changes. Even so, its business operations show resilience and stability with its prudent loan management and asset diversification. This is what we saw in its most recent performance. Before we continue, I’d like to highlight that all amounts in this article are in CAD.

In Q1 2026, its interest income amounted to $20.7B, down by 9.5% YoY from $22.9B. This was also lower than in my previous coverage at $21.4B. This downtrend still suggests that TD may need some time to recover. The impact of the policy easing cycle in the second half of 2025 drove this. After all, the US Fed and BoC have already slashed their interest rates to 3.75% and 2.25%, respectively. With that, the yields of TD’s interest-earning assets also decreased. You can see that interest on loans, investments, and deposits decreased despite their increased volume.

On a lighter note, the interest rate reduction also reduced the interest expense by 20.7%. As a result, its net interest income increased to $8.7B versus $7.9B YoY. Moreover, it opened some opportunities for TD to expand its market coverage and penetration. Its loans increased as lower interest rates attracted more borrowers. Even so, it stayed conservative with its credit card lending. Also, its business and government loans increased, which are more secure and stable. Because it accommodated more borrowers and other customers, it was also able to raise some of its noninterest income, such as service charges and insurance revenue. In the end, the interest rate cut allowed TD to generate more revenues while stabilizing expenses. Its net income margin even rose to 24.4% versus 19.9% YoY.

Areas Of Consideration

As a bank, TD is sensitive to inflation and monetary policy changes by central banks. Today, inflation has already slowed down but remains above the 2.0% target, which reflects its stubbornness. Also, positive inflation means prices are still increasing. New tariffs might also fuel this due to their tendency to raise domestic prices. As a result, purchasing power and borrowing repayment capacity may weaken. This may affect TD’s loan quality and deposit levels.

It must also watch out for the tension in the Middle East. Although TD is not immediately impacted by higher oil prices due to the nature of its business and geographical location, the multiplier effect must be considered. Oil is crucial in running the overall economy and may raise fuel prices as well. These two may heavily impact various industries, such as shipping, logistics, manufacturing, industrial, and even retail. This may spur inflation and pause the policy easing cycle.

Why TD Stays Well-Positioned

Despite the potential threats, I believe that TD stays strategic due to these aspects.

One thing I like about it is its strategic loan management and diversification. Its exposure across consumer, commercial, and mortgage loans remain well-balanced. But TD still prioritizes business and government loans, as they comprise 36.2% of the total amount. In my view, this is logical since these loans are typically secured and collateralized. Also, business owners are more capable of making time loan repayments than consumers, especially now that macroeconomic conditions are anticipated to be more volatile. This may help TD maintain decent credit quality and manage delinquencies. As you can see, gross impaired loans remain low at 0.27% on average. It is also worth noting that credit card loans decreased despite lower interest rates. This shows TD’s conservative approach to growth. It focused on safer or more secure loans to balance loan growth with quality.

It also stays conservative by keeping about $1.0B in provisions every quarter, which will serve as a buffer for potential bad debt in its US and Canadian personal and commercial lending business. This is crucial in ensuring its liquidity amid the looming macroeconomic threats discussed earlier.

In addition, the majority of its loans have fixed rates. For instance, the percentage of variable-rate Canadian mortgages maturing this year is only 21.0%. This is strategic since it may protect TD from macroeconomic volatility and sudden policy rate changes. Also, the recent policy easing cycle may still have an impact on variable loans, which can decrease their yields. As a result, TD may still manage loan yields at some levels. For the following years, especially in FY 2030, the percentage of variable-rate loans will increase based on the maturity schedule. But I think this also makes sense because the macroeconomic environment in Canada may have already stabilized by that time. So, interest rates are more stable, leading to more stable yields at a lower funding cost.

Lastly, TD’s interest-earning assets are still well-diversified. To be honest, I find it quite refreshing to see a bank not relying too heavily on loans to multiply its income or spur growth. As you can see, TD’s loans only comprise 48% of the total. Meanwhile, investment securities take 35% of the portion. Deposits and debt securities under reverse repurchase agreements get the remaining 17%. This shows that TD has well-balanced and multiple income streams. This is like putting eggs into different baskets. More importantly, this makes TD very liquid. In case of deposit runs, TD will not be hostage since investments are typically liquid assets. Additionally, its investments are well-diversified. The majority of its investments are trading securities, which can be easily withdrawn whenever TD wants to. It also has some good risk-reward exposure since it also has equities, which support its revenue growth. If you look at its income statement, TD generated $632M, a 20% YoY increase.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is now $3.16, so the dividend growth based on the five-year CAGR is also higher at 6.10%. Meanwhile, I increased its risk premium to 5.5% to be more conservative and price in more risks than the 5.0% standard. Lastly, my new Beta is 0.93. This means that TD has become less volatile than in my previous coverage and the S&P 500. With all this information, I estimated a TP or target price of $98.54. This is higher than the current price and my previous TP. But the $5 gap shows the TD is still nearly fully priced. Coupled with potential market risks, I advise investors to stay on sidelines.

Technicals

TD’s technicals have been flat in the past three months, which show its consolidation. This also shows its resilience to the recent market risks as selling pressures remain relatively low. Yet, you can see that the stock price is now trending slightly below the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is also quite weaker than the average in the past 50 days. Additionally, the 50-day SMA appears to be flattening, showing that recent market sentiments have neutralized or have become less optimistic.

Its MACD and RSI also signal caution. You can see how their trends look opposite to the price trend, which suggests bearish divergence. Also, the MACD line is decreasing below the signal line within negative territory along with the histogram, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Lastly, RSI is still decreasing and a bit far from being oversold, so it is challenging to spot an entry point.

Conclusion

Toronto-Dominion Bank has already cemented its position as one of the biggest publicly-traded Canadian banks. This is supported by its robust liquidity, disciplined lending management, and prudent asset management. However, valuation shows limited upside potential. Technicals also warrant caution as recent trend appears increasing selling volume and bearish tendencies. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Salesforce: AI Is Not The Problem, Architecture Is
data.description:
I am initiating coverage on Salesforce (NYSE: CRM) with a Hold rating. While I don't subscribe to the "SaaSpocalypse" theory—Salesforce’s core business model remains fundamentally insulated from total AI displacement—I am increasingly concerned by the structural friction hindering their ability to scale efficiently.

The market is currently witnessing a "Execution Gap" between Salesforce and its more nimble peers. While the company once effortlessly commanded 20%+ growth, we are now looking at a 9% reality with a management-guided 11% CAGR for the coming years. For the stock to see a meaningful multiple expansion, Salesforce needs to prove that 11% isn't just a ceiling, but a floor.

I have my doubts. As competitors prove more adept at generating immediate ROI for enterprise clients, Salesforce faces a difficult choice: continue the "platform-pivot" or risk a permanent slowdown. In this thesis, I will break down why their current strategy requires a massive overhaul to regain its status as a high-growth compounder.

Context

In FY2026, Salesforce reported USD 41.5 billion in total revenue, reflecting a 10% YoY growth (9% in constant currency). This landed squarely at the high end of their raised guidance range, signaling that the company is hitting its operational marks despite the broader "SaaSpocalypse" concerns. More impressive, however, was the cash flow story: Free Cash Flow (FCF) reached USD 14.4 billion, a robust 16% YoY increase, allowing the company to return over USD 14 billion to shareholders through repurchases and dividends.

These results are objectively strong for a company of this scale. In the Q4 FY2026 earnings call, CEO Marc Benioff struck a characteristically defiant tone, leaning into the company's resilience:

For the full year, we delivered $41.5 billion in revenue, up 10% year-over-year and 9% constant currency. We had $11.2 billion in revenue for the fourth quarter, up 12% year-over-year, 10% constant currency. CRPO rose to $35.1 billion, up 16% year-over-year and 13% in constant currency. And we passed an incredible milestone with $72 billion in total RPO, which is up 14% year-over-year. Now that is $72 billion in total RPO, up 14% year-over-year in case you missed that point. I did read a tweet that RPO does not matter, but evidently, we have it if it does matter. So total RPO, $72 billion.

Last year, management laid out a clear trajectory toward double-digit revenue growth by the second half of fiscal year 2027, and so far, they are hitting those internal benchmarks. Driven by a resilient Q4 performance and the surprisingly fast integration of the Informatica acquisition, Salesforce has officially moved the goalposts—updating its fiscal year 2030 revenue target to a massive USD 63 billion.

When CEO Benioff discusses the cRPO (current Remaining Performance Obligations), he’s highlighting the "backlog" of revenue already under contract for the next 12 months. It’s important to clarify that this isn't upfront cash, but rather a firm commitment to deliver roughly USD 35 billion in services over the coming year. This 12% YoY growth in obligations acts as a safety net, effectively securing the revenue floor for FY2027.

However, a long-term target of USD 63 billion requires more than just defensive stability; it requires a structural victory over the efficiency gap. To that end, it is critical to evaluate the specific roadmap CEO Benioff has set for 2030:

We are now targeting FY '30 revenue of $63 billion, which represents an 11% CAGR from FY '26 to FY '30. We remain on track to Rule of 50 by FY '30, and we are pleased that with our continued focus on operational excellence, we delivered 60 basis points of expansion in FY '26. As we think about FY '27 and fueling our framework, we are making targeted investments, including advancing our Hyperforce third-party infrastructure for trust and security, ramping our AE capacity and scaling FTEs to drive adoption. These investments are partially funded by efficiency we've unlocked becoming the lean agentic enterprise as our own customer zero.

Indeed, management is now anchoring its long-term narrative to a USD 63 billion revenue target for 2030. To hit that mark, Salesforce must maintain a steady 11% CAGR over the next four years—a task that sounds manageable on paper but becomes significantly more complex when you factor in the law of large numbers and the shifting competitive landscape.

My primary objective in this article is to stress-test the feasibility of this target against Salesforce's current market positioning. We are no longer in the era of easy "cloud migration" growth. To bridge the gap from USD 41.5 billion today to USD 63 billion in 2030, Salesforce isn't just selling software; they are betting the entire house on the "Agentic" pivot and the successful monetization of Data Cloud. In the sections below, I will break down the mechanics of this 11% CAGR and calculate the stock's intrinsic value based on these management projections.

Business Model

While Salesforce carved out its legacy as the pioneer of cloud-based CRM, the organization is currently navigating a high-stakes identity shift. We are seeing a move away from the traditional per-seat licensing model—the bread and butter of the SaaS era—toward a framework centered on autonomous digital labor. This isn't just a branding exercise; it is a fundamental bet that "agents" can eventually replace or augment the human workflows that have historically driven seat-count growth.

The goal here is to move beyond simple record-keeping. By attempting to unify its technical infrastructure, Salesforce is positioning itself to host autonomous AI agents capable of executing mid-level logic, such as resolving billing discrepancies or qualifying top-of-funnel leads, without manual intervention.

This platform-centric approach is designed to tackle the persistent problem of fragmented data silos. By pulling disparate information into a centralized layer, the objective is to move AI from a passive summarization tool to an active participant in business logic. For those holding the stock, the narrative is clear: Salesforce is attempting to transition from a utility for human employees into the underlying infrastructure for a company’s next generation of automated workers.

Over the last decade, Salesforce fueled its expansion through high-profile acquisitions including MuleSoft (2018), Tableau (2019), Slack (2021), and the pivotal Informatica deal in 2025. This aggressive M&A strategy was designed to bridge the gap between internal developments and external specialized tools, eventually culminating in the Agentforce 360 platform as we see it today.

Historically, Salesforce struggled with a reputation for maintaining a Frankenstein architecture. Before the launch of Agentforce 360, the platform was frequently criticized for its siloed ecosystem, where disparate software layers forced a heavy integration tax on enterprise clients. This fragmentation often diluted the ROI of the core CRM suite, as companies spent more on connecting the dots than on actually leveraging the data.

In the current 2026 landscape, it is vital to understand how these legacy acquisitions have been repurposed within the unified Agentforce framework. MuleSoft now provides the underlying agent fabric, acting as the orchestration layer that allows AI agents to execute actions within third-party environments like SAP or Oracle. Tableau has transitioned into the semantic layer, providing the necessary business logic to translate raw data into concepts that an autonomous agent can actually reason with and visualize.

The 2025 acquisition of Informatica represents the final piece of the data governance puzzle. It serves as the cleansing and mastering layer, ensuring that the enterprise data feeding the AI is high-fidelity—a critical step in preventing the hallucinations that plagued earlier iterations of generative AI. These components are tied together by Data Cloud, which Salesforce developed internally to serve as the real-time engine that resolves various customer signals into a single, unified profile for the agents to reference.

With the architectural foundation set, we can now look at the revenue breakdown to understand exactly how Salesforce is monetizing this agent-centric infrastructure:

To visualize the current monetization of Agentforce 360, it helps to think of the platform as an amusement park where the entrance fee is a mandatory prerequisite before any of the individual attractions can be accessed. This "gatekeeper" model is exactly how Salesforce is driving its top-line expansion today. For a client to deploy a specific tool like Agentforce Sales, they must first commit to the underlying Agentforce 360 infrastructure.

This tiered architecture explains the significant divergence in growth rates across their portfolio. While core CRM remains a mature, slower-growing category, the Agentforce 360 platform segment is surging at 22% YoY. It isn't just a product; it is the "toll bridge" that every enterprise must cross if they want to participate in Salesforce’s autonomous AI future.

Anthropic Is Not A Threat, It's A Partnership

It is a mistake to view Anthropic as a competitive threat to Salesforce; rather, it has become a strategic hedge. Much like the vertical integrations seen with ServiceNow or Nemetschek, Salesforce has locked in a foundational partnership with Anthropic’s Claude models. By embedding Claude directly into its "Trust Layer," Salesforce has effectively mitigated the risk of a commodity AI arms race.

This integration is particularly potent in regulated sectors. By positioning Claude as the "governed" choice for high-margin industries, Salesforce is successfully upselling the Agentforce 360 Platform to global banks and healthcare providers—entities that historically avoided generative AI due to data residency and security protocols. While this creates a seemingly stable runway for growth, the "hold" rating in my thesis stems from a deeper structural concern that many sell-side analysts are currently overlooking.

The primary challenge is the "Unit Economics of Automation." For nearly two decades, SaaS valuation was a function of seat-count expansion. However, as we enter the 2026–2027 cycle, we are seeing a clear secular decline in total seats as autonomous agents begin to absorb human workloads.

The reality is that most sell-side analysts are struggling to model the SaaS industry for the coming years. We are entering a cycle where the traditional "seat count" is expected to decline over time as automation takes hold:

The macroeconomic data suggests a cooling in traditional labor markets. As shown in the chart above, average monthly job growth in AI-affected sectors has been on a steady decline since 2023.

This is no longer a theoretical risk; for many enterprises, seat-count contraction is an active reality. Most sell-side analysts, operating with limited visibility into the specific AI conversion rates of individual SaaS players, have responded by slashing price targets across the sector. Their models are increasingly pricing in a "shrinking denominator"—the idea that there will simply be fewer human seats for these platforms to tax.

However, during the Q4 FY2026 earnings call, Salesforce CFO Robin Washington pushed back against this narrative, asserting that the platform's core seat count is not in a state of terminal decline:

Yes, I agree with that. I think we're monetizing AI, Keith, through many different fashions. We've got multiple ways to monetize. We're seeing great growth, as I mentioned, in our premium SKUs. We're seeing acceleration. I think just listening to the 3 customer interviews, it talks about the great value that they're getting from core. It's also important to point out, we didn't talk about it a lot, but our seats, we're still seeing them grow year-on-year and quarter-on-quarter.

So what we see is now with Agentforce with the system that you laid out, the system of agency, et cetera, we're just seeing incremental value to our software. And some of it's going to be consumption-based, but we're going to have a hybrid model. Seats will continue to be a key component of our growth going forward. And what we hope to see is just what you heard from the 3 customers today, incremental value coming as a result of our agentic technology and capabilities.

What we are likely witnessing is a strategic expansion of the Salesforce footprint beyond the traditional sales desk. The platform is capturing "seat share" from data analysts, marketers, and operations staff who previously operated outside the Salesforce ecosystem. Furthermore, the rollout of Agentforce is effectively creating a new persona: the AI Orchestrator.

This leads to a "consolidation effect" that can mask underlying headwinds. A client might retire 50 seats of a niche, third-party tool and consolidate that workflow by adding 30 seats to their Salesforce contract. From the perspective of CFO Robin Washington, this appears as net growth, even if the client's aggregate software spend has actually contracted. While I expect this specific growth lever to decelerate as the broader industry matures, the panic surrounding the "death of SaaS" feels significantly overstated.

Salesforce is already pivoting toward a hybrid monetization model. The inevitable pressure on seat-count growth can be structurally offset by a two-pronged strategy: aggressive pricing power on legacy seats and a high-margin consumption model for autonomous agents. In my view, the platform's ability to capture value will scale alongside the advancement of the AI itself.

Rather than fixating solely on the "AI disruption" narrative, there are more nuanced operational factors that investors should be monitoring.

The "Structural Reality" Title

After performing a deep dive into Salesforce’s 10-K filings spanning the last decade, the structural evolution of the business becomes much clearer. Moving past the top-line narrative, several key financial inflection points emerge that suggest a departure from their historical growth engine:

As the data in the table illustrates, Salesforce has managed to defend its gross margins at an elite level, consistently hovering around 77%. While this confirms a dominant market position and significant pricing power, it stands in stark contrast to a top-line growth narrative that is clearly cooling. Between 2017 and 2022, investors became accustomed to a 24%+ YoY expansion; however, we have seen a steady deceleration since that peak, with FY2026 revenue growth landing at a more modest 9.6%.

The historical Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) profile also tells a cautionary tale. For years, Salesforce struggled with an ROIC in the 1% to 3% range—a lackluster performance that I attribute directly to a bloated equity base resulting from years of stock-based compensation and dilutive M&A.

It is only in the last three fiscal years that we’ve seen a meaningful pivot, with ROIC finally scaling toward 8.5%. This improvement suggests that the "efficiency" era led by the C-suite is finally taking hold, though the challenge remains whether they can maintain this trajectory while their core growth engine shifts gears.

A closer look at the capital structure reveals a massive expansion in equity, ballooning from USD 15 billion in 2019 to USD 58 billion by 2022. This wasn't organic growth; it was a byproduct of the "growth-at-all-costs" era, primarily driven by shares issued for major acquisitions.

The aggressive onboarding of Tableau and Slack—funded through a heavy mix of stock and cash—has left a lasting mark on the balance sheet. In fact, goodwill now accounts for over 50% of total assets, a clear indicator of the high premium paid for past growth. This equity bloat was further exacerbated by consistent layers of stock-based compensation.

This structural inefficiency is precisely what drew the attention of activist investors, who successfully pivoted management toward operational discipline and aggressive capital return. The numbers speak for themselves: nearly USD 20 billion in share repurchases over the last three fiscal years (USD 4 billion in 2023, USD 7.6 billion in 2024, and USD 7.8 billion in 2025). While this is a massive effort to shrink the denominator, my concern remains the long-term value proposition.

I evaluate long-term compounding through a simple lens: Long-term compounding = f(Growth, ROIC, P/E). For a company to be a true compounder, it requires stable, preferably double-digit revenue growth and a high, consistent ROIC. The P/E multiple typically follows as a byproduct of these two drivers.

In Salesforce’s case, we are seeing a disconnect. Revenue growth has cooled to 9%, and even with management’s guided 11% CAGR, the "high-growth" narrative is under pressure. While the aggressive buyback program is a necessary tool to artificially boost ROIC, the improvement is gradual. If the market expects 9-11% growth paired with a mid-teen ROIC, there is little fundamental reason for the P/E multiple to expand materially. The real question for investors is whether Salesforce can find a way to consistently surpass that 11% ceiling; without that acceleration, the stock lacks the necessary catalyst for a major rerating.

The Integration Costs For A Client: A Material Risk For Salesforce

A critical factor that most investors overlook is the structural friction embedded in Salesforce’s growth history. While competitors like ServiceNow were built on a single, modular codebase from day one, Salesforce has expanded through a decade of aggressive, disparate acquisitions. Integrating Slack (messaging), Tableau (analytics), MuleSoft (middleware), and most recently Informatica (data management) into a cohesive ecosystem is not a simple "plug-and-play" exercise.

Each of these acquisitions brought its own database architecture and metadata language. Because the core "Clouds" (Sales, Service, Marketing) were not designed to share a native heart, the company is now forced to build expensive, complex bridges just to make AI functional. The very necessity of the Informatica acquisition in 2025 serves as an indictment of this fragmentation; if the platform were truly unified, Salesforce wouldn't need to spend billions on a tool specifically designed to clean, move, and "master" messy data.

To make Agentforce 360 work, enterprises are often forced to physically ingest or duplicate data into Data Cloud. This creates a "Data Duplication Tax"—clients essentially pay for storage twice while maintaining complex pipelines to keep that data in sync. If these pipelines fail, the AI’s reasoning becomes unreliable, grounded in stale or "hallucinated" data records.

In contrast, ServiceNow’s "Single Platform" architecture operates on a modular "switch" logic. When a client needs a new capability, the module is simply toggled on within the existing environment. There is no need for expensive integrations or data replication because the data never left the platform. This architectural elegance leads to a faster ROI for the client and higher margins for the provider.

Having benchmarked ServiceNow’s efficiency first, the contrast in Salesforce's execution becomes clear. Even a "win" in the Salesforce ecosystem highlights this friction. During the Q4 FY2026 call, management celebrated SharkNinja standing up an agent in eight weeks—a timeline that, in a truly unified, modular environment, would be considered a lag rather than a milestone. This "Integration Tax" is what a major Salesforce client alluded to in the recent Q4 call:

Mark Adam Barrocas

SharkNinja, Inc.

Look, Marc, I mean, we launched 25 products a year, and we're really innovating at speed. And we need customer service solutions that move just as fast. I mean most companies treat service as a cost center. For us, Marc, it's really about lifetime value of a consumer. I mean we view service as a growth engine for the business. And it's not just about servicing problems, it's about building lifetime value.

We set up with you and your team, a guided shopping agent in 8 weeks right before the holiday season. I was nervous about it as I went to my team and I said, we're putting this in place in October. There's generally kind of a cutoff in our business where after October 1, you don't really do anything. And we launched this in 8 weeks, and it brought tremendous value to the consumer. I mean, it helped them with researching and buying and troubleshooting really all in one seamless conversation. So it was a great success for us this holiday season.

The friction in Salesforce’s architecture is most visible when looking at time-to-value. During the Q4 FY2026 call, management highlighted a client implementation that took eight weeks to deploy a guided shopping agent—a pre-built tool within the Agentforce (formerly Einstein) ecosystem. While Salesforce frames this as a success, it looks sluggish when benchmarked against the competition. In 2025 and early 2026, ServiceNow documented multiple retail and utility clients deploying Now Assist agents for customer service in just 14 to 21 days.

This leads to the primary reason I am wary of Salesforce's long-term dominance: ServiceNow is effectively "invading" the front office from the back.

In the current market, enterprises are no longer satisfied with a CRM that simply records a customer complaint; they demand immediate resolution. Because ServiceNow already controls the operational "organs" of the company—IT, HR, and supply chain—their Customer Service Management (CSM) can trigger a back-end fix, such as a refund or a technician dispatch, almost instantly. Salesforce, by contrast, must build a bridge via Data Cloud or MuleSoft just to reach those same internal systems.

The financial divergence is already showing up in the numbers. Salesforce is fighting to maintain a 9.6% YoY growth rate, while ServiceNow continues to scale at north of 20%. My view is that surpassing the guided 11% CAGR will require a Herculean effort from Salesforce. Until they can prove that their "Integration Tax" isn't a permanent drag on client ROI, it is difficult to justify a higher multiple for the stock.

Valuation

According to SA Quant ratings, the stock appears attractive, as several valuation multiples suggest Salesforce is trading at a discount relative to its primary peers.

To gain more granular control over the underlying projections, let's move toward a valuation model based on the following specific assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 956 million

  • FCF Margins: 24% (representing the 10-year historical average)

  • Holding Period: I am assuming the stock is held through the year 2030.

  • Revenue Growth: 11% CAGR (consistent with management’s long-term guidance)

  • Terminal P/FCF (2030): 18x. While the P/FCF fluctuated between 23x and 26x over the last four years, it dipped toward 14x in FY2026. I am modeling 18x on the assumption that Salesforce rebuilds investor confidence as it executes on its 2030 targets.

  • Discount rate: 9%

Based on management’s recent guidance, I have modeled revenue projections through 2030, reaching an estimated USD 63 billion. This trajectory implies a compound annual growth rate of 11%. By applying a forecasted 24% FCF margin to these top-line figures, we arrive at an estimated 2030 free cash flow of USD 15.1 billion.

To determine the per-share value, I divided that USD 15.1 billion by the 956 million shares currently outstanding, resulting in an FCF per share of USD 15.80 for 2030. Applying an 18x multiple to this figure yields a 2030 target price of USD 284.80. To find the current fair value, I discounted this target back to 2026.

The intrinsic value is calculated as follows:

Intrinsic value = 284.8/(1+discounted rate)^4.

This calculation produces an approximate intrinsic value of USD 198 per share, suggesting that the current stock price offers no meaningful margin of safety. From a strategic standpoint, Salesforce may face significant headwinds in delivering that 11% annual growth. Its platform architecture often necessitates higher integration costs and lacks the operational efficiency seen in competitors like ServiceNow.

Risk

The primary risk to my thesis is the possibility that Salesforce successfully reduces integration costs for its clients in the coming years. By combining a more aggressive sales posture and higher customer satisfaction levels with robust share buybacks to sustain ROIC, the company could achieve performance that surpasses current projections. This scenario could drive annual revenue growth north of 11% and push ROIC toward 15%, likely resulting in an expansion of the stock’s P/E multiple.

Nevertheless, I maintain that the odds are not in Salesforce's favor, as competition has reached unprecedented levels. The firm must battle players like ServiceNow, which already possesses a streamlined architecture capable of scaling at a faster clip. While I am not concerned about the immediate AI threat—given that Salesforce has effectively fortified its ecosystem through a strategic partnership with Anthropic—the rapid advancement of other industry peers remains the chief concern. Ultimately, the consequences of a mismanaged growth strategy could lead to slower-than-expected expansion, triggering a downward revision in long-term expectations and hindering the stock's appreciation potential.

Takeaways

Salesforce’s strategic alliance with Anthropic suggests that the sensationalist "SaaSapocalypse" narrative is likely the least of the company's structural worries. The real pressure comes from peers like ServiceNow, whose superior scaling strategy is evidenced by consistent revenue growth exceeding 20% in recent years.

To remain competitive, Salesforce must prove that its hybrid model of aggressive acquisitions and internal R&D can deliver a superior ROI per dollar of integration cost relative to its more streamlined rivals. My previous deep dive into ServiceNow’s architecture highlighted a more efficient scaling mechanism that offers clients a clearer path to value.

Beyond horizontal competition, Salesforce faces significant friction from niche specialists like Veeva Systems in the healthcare sector—a topic I’ve covered extensively. Ultimately, Salesforce will need to exert considerable effort to outperform its 11% CAGR guidance if it hopes to restore long-term market confidence.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Inflation Protected Securities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

In the US, we expect a continued expansion of global economic growth in 2026, with most major economies contributing to above-trend growth. Activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the "One Big Beautiful Bill". We expect the Fed to cut rates further in 2026, but above-trend growth argues for only limited additional easing. That said, the appointment of a dovish new Fed Chair could point to somewhat more room for cuts, depending on the extent to which the new Chair manages to convince the rest of the Federal Open Market Committee of their policy views.

Inflation: The all items index rose 2.7 percent for the 12 months ending December, the same increase as over the 12 months ending November. The all items less food and energy index rose 2.6 percent over the last 12 months. The energy index increased 2.3 percent for the 12 months ending December. The food index increased 3.1 percent over the last year.

Growth: We expect a continued expansion of global economic growth in 2026, with most major economies contributing to above-trend growth. In the US, activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the "One Big Beautiful Bill". Global economies may face near-term headwinds from tariffs but remain supported over the medium term due to robust private sector fundamentals, looser and expansive monetary and fiscal policy, and accelerated AI capital expenditures. Heading into 2026, we believe growth in the US faces potential drag from trade and immigration, but risks may be mitigated by policy easing, fiscal stimulus, and prospective AI investments. Economies outside the US, such as the Euro area and Japan, are also expected to remain resilient, aided by easing monetary policy, expansive fiscal stimulus, and trade.

The Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index returned -0.40% in December and the Intermediate TIPS (1-10yrs) Index returned -0.19%. This is compared to the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index, which returned -0.33% and the Intermediate Treasury Index, which returned 0.67%.

Performance and Attribution

The Fund's I-shares returned 0.16% net over the fourth quarter and outperformed the benchmark by 2bps.

The Fund's specific government and yield curve trades contributed to performance, primarily driven by our US 2y3y Steepener and US 2y5y Steepener. The US Yield Curve steepened in Q4'25, as the Fed continued its cutting cycle. Short-term yields trended downwards as long-term yields remained elevated due to inflation concerns. Additionally, elevated term premia likely driven by fiscal and institutional risks kept long-term yields elevated as well. Spreads between short-term yields and long-term yields widened. This widening between the short-end and long-end led to our steepeners outperforming.

Strategy/Outlook

Multiple factors could impact economic conditions and financial markets in 2026, making it challenging to predict a clear direction. We believe active investment management is well-suited for navigating such conditions. An investment approach that goes beyond simply following benchmarks is important heading into 2026, in our view, especially with asset valuations elevated. This may involve actively managing portfolios and tilting towards areas where we see potential value and income opportunities, such as small-cap equities and securitized credit. We believe diversification across alternative assets can enhance risk-adjusted returns.

Labor market dynamics hold the key to Federal Reserve (Fed) easing into 2026, in our view, so long as inflation remains anchored. As tariffs settle at lower (but not low) levels than initially seemed likely, we believe they still present a downside risk to growth. Global government debt has surged past $100 trillion, and the fiscal backdrop is a key concern that could trigger market volatility.

Policy: We believe the labor market holds the key to the pace and scale of Fed easing into 2026, so long as inflation remains anchored. If labor market weakness persists, driven by immigration restrictions, federal layoffs, and labor-saving AI, additional Fed rate cuts may follow, especially if tariff-induced inflation is short-lived. We expect the ECB to maintain its 2% rate, given at-target inflation and German fiscal expansion, though we do not rule out a return to rate cuts if inflation begins to moderate. In the UK, we believe easing inflation, a softening labor market, and fiscal contraction support further easing. Disinflation, Fed cuts, and signs of further dollar weakness could lead to further easing in emerging markets, with country variations. Japan remains on track towards a higher rate regime, in our view, backed by firm inflation, strong GDP growth, and potential fiscal easing. With policy rates becoming less restrictive and no tariff-induced recession, rate hikes in other economies are also possible, signaling a new phase in global monetary policy.

We expect fiscal policy to remain in focus in 2026, with a new economic package in Japan, federal spending accelerating in Germany and potential new measures to be announced in the US ahead of the Midterm elections.

Overall, our current TIPS positioning is slightly overweight vs. the benchmark (being underweight to shorter-dated TIPS and overweight to longer-dated TIPS), and we maintain long-term conviction in the asset class. In today's market, TIPS offer historically elevated real yields and inflation protected principal. This is powerful in providing investors high levels of expected return, income protection in an uncertain inflationary environment, and diversification benefits in fixed income portfolios.

Fund Performance

For periods one year or greater, performance is annualized. The returns represent past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The Fund's investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit Financial Intermediaries to obtain the most recent month-end returns. Performance reflects cumulative total returns for periods of less than one year and average annual total returns for periods of greater than one year. All Fund performance data reflect the reinvestment of distributions.

Standardized Total Returns for Period Ended 12/31/25

Class A Shares Class I Shares
One Year 2.55% 7.01%
Five Years -0.12% 0.97%
Ten Years 2.16% 2.90%

The Standardized Total Returns are average annual total returns or cumulative total returns (only if the performance period is one year or less) as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. They assume reinvestment of all distributions at net asset value. These returns reflect the maximum initial sales charge of 3.75% for Class A Shares. Because Institutional Shares do not involve a sales charge, such a charge is not applied to their Standardized Total Returns.

Expense Ratios for Period Ended 12/31/25

Current Expense Ratio (Net) Expense Ratio Before Waivers (Gross)
Class A Shares 0.67% 0.82%
Class I Shares 0.34% 0.49%

The expense ratios of the Fund, both current (net of any fee waivers or expense limitations) and before waivers (gross of any fee waivers or expense limitations) are as set forth above. Pursuant to a contractual arrangement, the Fund's waivers and/or expense limitations will remain in place through at least July 29, 2026 and prior to such date the investment adviser may not terminate the arrangements without the approval of the Fund's Board of Trustees. Please refer to the Fund's prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Definition of Terms

Bps: basis points, or 1/100th of 1%

CAI: Goldman Sachs Asset Management's Current Activity Indicator (CAI) offer a real-time economic snapshot by analyzing over 300 high-frequency activity data points, expressed in GDP terms. These tools enable us to quickly identify economic shifts and adapt our investment strategies accordingly

Bps: basis points, or 1/100th

CPI: Consumer Price Index

PCE: Personal Consumption Expenditure Price Index

PPI: Producer Price Index

TIPS: an acronym for Treasury Inflation-Protected Security

TIPS Breakeven: calculated by subtracting the yield of an inflation-protected bond from the yield of a nominal bond

Hawkish (monetary policy): monetary policy focused on controlling inflation by either raising interest rates or keeping interest rates at elevated levels

Dovish (monetary policy): tends to suggest lower interest rates; opposite of hawkish

FOMC: Federal Open Market Committee

Source: Bloomberg, Goldman Sachs Asset Management. As of 31-Dec-2025.

Risk Considerations:

The Goldman Sachs Inflation Protected Securities Fund invests primarily in inflation protected securities (IPS) of varying maturities issued by the U.S. Treasury and other U.S. and non-U.S. Government agencies and corporations. Fixed income securities are subject to the risks associated with debt securities generally, including credit, liquidity and interest rate risk. IPS are fixed income securities whose interest and principal payments are periodically adjusted according to the rate of inflation. The market value of IPS is not guaranteed, and will fluctuate in response to changes in real interest rates. The market for IPS may be less developed or liquid, and more volatile, than certain other securities markets. If deflation were to occur, IPS would likely decline in price. Any guarantee on U.S. government securities applies only to the underlying securities of the Fund if held to maturity and not to the value of the Fund's shares. The Fund's investments are also subject to market risk, which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. Derivative instruments may involve a high degree of financial risk. These risks include the risk that a small movement in the price of the underlying security or benchmark may result in a disproportionately large movement, unfavorable or favorable, in the price of the derivative instrument; the risk of default by a counterparty; and liquidity risk (i.e., the risk that an investment may not be able to be sold without a substantial drop in price, if at all). The Fund's investments in other investment companies (including ETFs) subject it to additional expenses.

General Disclosures:

Fund holdings and allocations may not include the Fund's entire investment portfolio, which may change at any time. Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Economic and market forecasts presented herein reflect our judgment as of the date of this presentation and are subject to change without notice. These forecasts do not take into account the specific investment objectives, restrictions, tax and financial situation or other needs of any specific client. Actual data will vary and may not be reflected here. These forecasts are subject to high levels of uncertainty that may affect actual performance. Accordingly, these forecasts should be viewed as merely representative of a broad range of possible outcomes. Views and opinions are current as of the date of this commentary and may be subject to change, they should not be construed as investment advice. Consumer Price Index (CPI) is measured by the changes in the price level of a market basket of consumer goods and services purchased by households.

The "core" PCE price index is defined as Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) prices excluding food and energy prices. The core PCE price index measures the prices paid by consumers for goods and services without the volatility caused by movements in food and energy prices to reveal underlying inflation trends.

Producer Price Index (PPI) is measured by the average changes in prices received by domestic producers for their output.

data.title: Airtel Africa: Strong Operating Momentum, But Valuation Now Looks Balanced
data.description:
Introduction

Airtel Africa (AAFRF) has been delivering strong performance and operating results over the last year. Indeed, the stock is up more than 130% over the last twelve months. Although this has consequences for the valuation, with the stock outperforming underlying EBITDA growth over the same period, the underlying metrics are genuinely good, with increases in constant currency revenue and profitability, as well as expanding margins on declining leverage. The planned Airtel Money listing this year could be a real strategic catalyst, but the stock is still very sensitive to currency translation risk. I rate Airtel Africa a Hold.

Improving Operating Metrics

In the last earnings report, for Q3 2026, the numbers looked strong. Overall revenue increased by 28.3% to $4.67 billion, while revenue rose by 24.6% in constant currency. Growth was particularly strong in Nigeria, which continues to account for a large share of incremental group revenue.

Things looked just as good on the profitability side, with EBITDA up nearly 36% to $2.28 billion on top of a 2.7 point increase in EBITDA margin to hit 48.9%, which is a genuinely impressive number for a telecommunications operator. The drivers for this increase were mostly due to scale, FX translation, and a higher contribution of Mobile Money, which achieved an EBITDA margin of 51%. All this also helped increase operating free cash flow by around 37%.

Mobile Money, an increasingly important segment for the company now good for more than 20% of revenue, reached two major milestones this quarter, according to management. Firstly, the number of subscribers topped 50 million for the first time, up 17.3% to 52 million. Secondly, the total processed value reached over $200 billion, increasing 36% to more than $210 billion. This reinforces the view that Airtel Africa is increasingly a hybrid telecom-fintech platform. This higher-margin revenue mix also supports group margin resilience as legacy voice declines.

Airtel Africa operates in a growing market. Smart phone penetration rose by 3.9% to 48.1%, giving the company plenty of room to increase its customer base. Indeed, data customers were up 14.6%, and perhaps more importantly, customers are buying more data, up to 8.6GB from 6.9GB. Rising consumption provides a pathway to future ARPU expansion even if subscriber growth slows.

FX Sensitivity and Capital Structure

Part of the reason the company’s performance over the last year looks so good is thanks to positive FX translation effects. The Nigerian Naira has appreciated from around 1545 to the dollar to around 1360 for an increase of 12%, making its results look better in dollar terms. In contrast, prior periods saw earnings pressure from FX losses. This tailwind is a double edged sword and the biggest risk to take into account when evaluating Airtel Africa. Its geographical concentration in Nigeria and sensitivity to the Naira, a relatively volatile emerging markets currency, can cut both ways. As a result, equity performance may diverge substantially from underlying operating performance.

On the balance sheet side, risk seems to be decreasing somewhat. Overall leverage decreased to 1.9x net debt/EBITDA, down from 2.4x. However, the telecommunications model has structurally high capital intensity. CAPEX rose from around $490 million to $670 million, largely as a result of accelerated network rollout and increased investments in data capacity. However, the fact that the company managed to increase operating FCF significantly despite higher CAPEX shows genuine earnings strength.

A Realistic Valuation

Due to the stock’s large increase over the last year, enterprise multiples have expanded alongside the rally, reducing the margin of safety relative to recent years. As earnings-based metrics are not particularly useful due to FX volatility, we’ll focus on other valuations. The company currently trades at around 9.3x trailing EV/EBITDA and 7.5x forward EV/EBITDA, which is considerably more expensive than more mature telecommunications peer Telefonica (TEF), but also based on much higher growth figures. Airtel’s 5-year revenue CAGR of around 10% and 3-year EBITDA CAGR of 4% are in a different league than Telefonica’s -4% and -11% respectively.

Price/Cash Flow of around 6x, while not cheap, indicates improving cash flow generation capacity, while EV/Sales of around 4x is again not cheap but more or less fair considering the growth prospects. Considering that margins are already very high, they are unlikely to cause a rerating, and upside is therefore increasingly dependent on FX stability, an improving balance sheet, and Airtel Money monetization.

Risks & Catalysts

As mentioned, the primary risk is that the Naira renews depreciation against the dollar, which is further amplified by the company’s geographic concentration in Nigeria, which comes with its own macro-economic and regulatory risks. At the same time, the sector’s high capital intensity requires sustained investments in network and spectrum, which limits immediate upside. Meanwhile, rising competition could put pressure on ARPU and margins.

In terms of catalysts, the clearest one on the horizon is the Airtel Money listing. A confirmation of the timeline, structure, and valuation range would give investors something to hold on to. In the absence of this, the market would probably benefit from a clearer segment breakout. As deleveraging continues, the balance sheet looks better, and improving free cash flow would enable higher shareholder returns. As Mobile Money continues to grow, margins may expand, but it looks like at 51%, further margin expansion may slow from already elevated levels.

Conclusion

Due to the sustained rally, Airtel Africa’s valuation looks fully priced, which is why I’m hesitant to go with a Buy rating. Otherwise, the underlying operational metrics look very good and may continue to provide upside in absence of a major depreciation of the naira versus the dollar. The major upside catalyst is the listing of Airtel Money, but until more details on this emerge, it’s hard to estimate what the exact consequences will be. Risk and reward currently look more or less balanced, and investors will continue to look towards things like FX movements and improved margins to sustain further stock price appreciation.
